1:
"Behold, what manner of love the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called the sons of God: therefore the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not." The witnesses note the Father's wondrous love that makes us his children. On "what manner of love the Father hath bestowed upon us," the grammatical voices note the marvel. Barnes and Robertson note the exclamation of wonder: "what manner of (potapen — what kind, how great and strange) love the Father hath bestowed (given, lavished) upon us, that we should be called the sons (children) of God" — John calls the readers to behold the astonishing, unprecedented love of the Father, that we should be named God's children.

On "that we should be called the sons of God," the witnesses note the dignity. Barnes and Calvin note that to be "called the sons of God" is the highest dignity — not merely named but truly made God's children; the love that adopts sinners into God's family is beyond all measure (some texts add "and we are," stressing the reality).

On "the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not," the puritan Henry notes the consequence: "therefore the world knoweth us not (does not recognize or acknowledge us as God's children), because it knew him not" — the world fails to recognize believers as God's children because it did not recognize Christ; the world's ignorance of Christ explains its ignorance of his people. The verse displays the Father's wondrous love that makes us his children.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the marvel — "what manner of (how great and strange) love the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called the sons (children) of God," the astonishing love that names us God's children (Barnes, Robertson); to be "called the sons of God" the highest dignity, truly made his children (Barnes, Calvin); "the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not," the world failing to recognize believers as it failed to recognize Christ (Henry). The verse displays the bestowed love. The verse marks the love: what manner. The verse displays the gift: sons of God. The verse displays the dignity: God's children. The verse displays the consequence: the world knows us not. The love displays the Father adopting us as his children. The verse displays the love: bestowed. The verse displays the sons: of God. The verse displays the world: ignorant. The Father's wondrous love that makes us his children displays the marvel that opens the chapter: behold what astonishing love the Father has lavished, that we should be called the sons of God — a dignity the world does not recognize, because it did not recognize him. The verse displays the children of God, awaiting their fuller revealing (v.2).
2:
"Beloved, now are we the sons of God, and it doth not yet appear what we shall be: but we know that, when he shall appear, we shall be like him; for we shall see him as he is." The witnesses note the present sonship and the future likeness to Christ. On "now are we the sons of God, and it doth not yet appear what we shall be," the grammatical voices note the present and the hidden future. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "now (already) are we the sons (children) of God, and it doth not yet appear (has not yet been made manifest) what we shall be" — believers are already God's children, but the full glory of their future state is not yet revealed; the present reality with the veiled future.

On "when he shall appear, we shall be like him," the witnesses note the coming likeness. Barnes and Calvin note the promise: "when he shall appear (at Christ's coming, or when it shall appear), we shall be like him (made like Christ)" — at Christ's return, believers shall be transformed into his likeness; the glorification that completes their sonship.

On "for we shall see him as he is," the eastern and grammatical-historical note the beatific vision: "for we shall see him as he is" — the cause of the transformation is the sight of Christ as he is; the beatific vision transforms the beholder into his likeness; to see Christ in his glory is to be made like him. The verse displays the present sonship and the future likeness to Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the present and future — "now are we the sons of God, and it doth not yet appear what we shall be," believers already God's children, the full glory not yet revealed (Barnes, Robertson); "when he shall appear, we shall be like him," transformed into Christ's likeness at his coming (Barnes, Calvin); "for we shall see him as he is," the beatific vision transforming the beholder, seeing Christ making us like him (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sonship and likeness. The verse marks the present: now sons of God. The verse displays the future: not yet revealed. The verse displays the likeness: like him. The verse displays the cause: see him as he is. The promise displays the children glorified by the sight of Christ. The verse displays the sons: now. The verse displays the likeness: to come. The verse displays the vision: of Christ. The present sonship and the future likeness to Christ display the believer's hope: we are already God's children, though what we shall be is not yet revealed — but when Christ appears we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is. The verse displays the transforming hope, which purifies its holder (v.3).
3:
"And every man that hath this hope in him purifieth himself, even as he is pure." The brief verse notes the purifying power of the hope of seeing Christ. The grammatical voices note the moral effect of hope. Barnes and Robertson note the practical fruit: "every man that hath this hope in him (the hope of being made like Christ, v.2) purifieth himself (cleanses himself from sin), even as he (Christ) is pure" — the hope of future likeness to Christ moves the believer to present self-purification; he seeks to be pure now, as Christ is pure.

On "purifieth himself," the witnesses note the active pursuit. Barnes and Calvin note that the believer "purifieth himself" — not that he sanctifies himself apart from grace, but that, moved by hope, he actively pursues holiness, cleansing himself from defilement; hope is no idle expectation but a sanctifying power.

On "even as he is pure," the puritan Henry notes the pattern: the standard is "even as he (Christ) is pure" — Christ's own purity is the model; the believer aims to be pure as Christ is pure; the hope of being like him in glory drives the effort to be like him in holiness now. The verse displays the purifying power of the hope of seeing Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the moral effect — "every man that hath this hope in him purifieth himself, even as he is pure," the hope of likeness to Christ moving the believer to self-purification (Barnes, Robertson); "purifieth himself," actively pursuing holiness, moved by hope, not apart from grace (Barnes, Calvin); "even as he is pure," Christ's purity the model, hope driving the effort to be like him now (Henry). The verse displays the purifying hope. The verse marks the hope: in him. The verse displays the effect: purifies himself. The verse displays the pattern: as Christ is pure. The verse displays the power: hope sanctifies. The hope displays the believer cleansing himself after Christ's purity. The verse displays the hope: held. The verse displays the purity: pursued. The verse displays the pattern: Christ. The purifying power of the hope of seeing Christ displays the sanctifying fruit of the believer's hope: everyone who has this hope of being made like Christ purifies himself, even as Christ is pure — the hope of future likeness driving present holiness. The verse displays the purifying hope, set against the nature of sin (v.4).
4:
"Whosoever committeth sin transgresseth also the law: for sin is the transgression of the law." The witnesses note the definition of sin as the transgression of the law. On "whosoever committeth sin transgresseth also the law," the grammatical voices note the equation. Barnes and Robertson note the description of sin's nature: "whosoever committeth (practices, poiei) sin transgresseth (poiei anomian — also does lawlessness, breaks the law)" — to commit sin is to break God's law; sin and lawlessness are one; every sin is a transgression of the divine law.

On "sin is the transgression of the law," the witnesses note the definition. Barnes and Calvin note the famous definition: "sin is the transgression of the law (anomia — lawlessness)" — sin is defined as lawlessness, the violation of God's law; whatever is contrary to the law of God is sin; the law is the standard by which sin is measured.

On the bearing, the puritan Henry notes the seriousness: by defining sin as lawlessness, John exposes the gravity of sin — it is not a trifle but rebellion against God's law; this exposes the false teachers who made light of sin, and underscores why the children of God must not practice it. The verse displays the definition of sin as the transgression of the law.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the equation — "whosoever committeth (practices) sin transgresseth (also does lawlessness) the law," to sin is to break God's law, sin and lawlessness one (Barnes, Robertson); "sin is the transgression of the law (lawlessness)," the definition of sin as violation of God's law, the standard (Barnes, Calvin); the definition exposing the gravity of sin as rebellion, against the false teachers who made light of it (Henry). The verse displays the nature of sin. The verse marks the sinning: committing. The verse displays the transgression: of the law. The verse displays the definition: lawlessness. The verse displays the gravity: rebellion. The definition displays sin as the breaking of God's law. The verse displays the sin: lawless. The verse displays the law: transgressed. The verse displays the standard: God's law. The definition of sin as the transgression of the law displays the seriousness John presses on the children of God: whoever commits sin breaks the law, for sin is lawlessness itself — no trifle but rebellion against God's standard. The verse displays the lawless nature of sin, against which Christ was manifested (v.5).
5:
"And ye know that he was manifested to take away our sins; and in him is no sin." The brief verse notes that Christ was manifested to take away sins, being himself sinless. The grammatical voices note the purpose of Christ's coming. Barnes and Robertson note the twofold truth: "ye know that he was manifested (appeared, was revealed in the flesh) to take away (remove, bear away) our sins" — Christ came expressly to take away sin, both its guilt and its power; the purpose of his manifestation was the removal of sin.

On "to take away our sins," the witnesses note the work. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ "takes away our sins" — by his atonement he removes the guilt of sin, and by his Spirit its dominion; the whole purpose of his coming was against sin; therefore those who belong to him cannot live in the sin he came to destroy.

On "in him is no sin," the grammatical-historical note Christ's sinlessness: "in him is no sin" — Christ himself is wholly without sin; the sinless one alone could take away sin; his perfect purity qualifies him to remove sin and is the pattern for his people. The verse displays that Christ was manifested to take away sins, being himself sinless.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose — "he was manifested to take away (remove) our sins," Christ coming expressly to take away sin, guilt and power (Barnes, Robertson); "to take away our sins," removing guilt by atonement and dominion by his Spirit, his coming wholly against sin (Barnes, Calvin); "in him is no sin," Christ himself sinless, qualified to take away sin, the pattern for his people (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sin-taking Christ. The verse marks the manifestation: to take away sin. The verse displays the work: removal of sin. The verse displays the sinlessness: no sin in him. The verse displays the pattern: the sinless one. The purpose displays Christ coming to destroy the sin he is free from. The verse displays the manifestation: against sin. The verse displays the sins: taken away. The verse displays the Christ: sinless. That Christ was manifested to take away sins, being himself sinless, displays the ground of the believer's break with sin: Christ appeared expressly to take away our sins, and in him is no sin — so those who belong to the sinless sin-bearer cannot live in the sin he came to destroy. The verse displays the sinless sin-bearer, whose people abide in him and sin not (v.6).
6:
"Whosoever abideth in him sinneth not: whosoever sinneth hath not seen him, neither known him." The witnesses note that whoever abides in Christ does not practice sin — touching the perfection crux. On "whosoever abideth in him sinneth not," interpreters note the sense (with v.9). The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with the Reformed Calvin, hold that "sinneth not (hamartanei — present tense, does not go on sinning, does not practice sin)" means the one who abides in Christ does not continue in sin, does not make a practice of sin; the linear present marks habitual conduct, not absolute sinlessness (reconciled with 1:8, "if we say we have no sin we deceive ourselves"). To abide in Christ and habitually sin are incompatible.

On "whosoever sinneth hath not seen him, neither known him," the witnesses note the test. Barnes and Calvin note that "whosoever sinneth (practices sin) hath not seen him, neither known him" — the one who lives in sin gives proof that he has never truly seen or known Christ; a life of sin betrays the absence of real knowledge of Christ; knowing Christ and practicing sin cannot coexist.

On the "abiding," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the condition: while one abides in Christ, sin has no dominion; the abiding in Christ is the secret of victory over sin; departure into sin shows a failure to abide. The verse displays that whoever abides in Christ does not practice sin.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree the substance: abiding in Christ and a life of sin are incompatible; the one who practices sin shows he has not truly known Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley). The grammatical and Reformed voices stress that "sinneth not" is the habitual present (does not practice sin), reconciled with 1:8; the Wesleyan voice stresses the real victory over sin in abiding. The verse displays the abiding that does not sin. The verse marks the abiding: in him. The verse displays the result: sinneth not. The verse displays the test: the sinner has not known him. The verse displays the sense: does not practice sin. The teaching displays abiding in Christ excluding the practice of sin. The verse displays the abiding: sinless walk. The verse displays the sinner: not knowing Christ. The verse displays the knowing: incompatible with sin. That whoever abides in Christ does not practice sin displays the incompatibility of sin with knowing Christ: the one who abides in him does not go on sinning, and whoever practices sin shows he has neither seen nor known him — the present tense marking habitual conduct, reconciled with the confession that we are not sinless (1:8). The verse displays the abiding that breaks with sin, against all deceivers (v.7).
7:
"Little children, let no man deceive you: he that doeth righteousness is righteous, even as he is righteous." The brief verse notes the warning that only the doer of righteousness is righteous. The grammatical voices note the test against deceivers. Barnes and Robertson note the caution: "little children, let no man deceive you (lead you astray)" — John warns against deceivers (likely those who taught that one could be righteous without righteous living); the test of true righteousness is set against their error.

On "he that doeth righteousness is righteous," the witnesses note the practical proof. Barnes and Calvin note the criterion: "he that doeth (practices) righteousness is righteous" — true righteousness is shown by doing right; not profession or knowledge but practice proves one righteous; righteousness is known by its fruit.

On "even as he is righteous," the puritan Henry notes the pattern: the standard is "even as he (Christ) is righteous" — Christ's own righteousness is the model; the one who does righteousness is righteous after the pattern of Christ; only such a life evidences true righteousness, against the deceivers' claims. The verse displays the warning that only the doer of righteousness is righteous.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the caution — "let no man deceive you," warning against deceivers who severed righteousness from righteous living (Barnes, Robertson); "he that doeth (practices) righteousness is righteous," practice not profession proving one righteous (Barnes, Calvin); "even as he is righteous," Christ's righteousness the pattern, against the deceivers (Henry). The verse displays the test of righteousness. The verse marks the warning: let none deceive you. The verse displays the criterion: doing righteousness. The verse displays the proof: righteous by practice. The verse displays the pattern: as Christ. The warning displays righteousness proved by doing, not claiming. The verse displays the deceivers: resisted. The verse displays the righteous: by doing. The verse displays the pattern: Christ. The warning that only the doer of righteousness is righteous displays the test against the deceivers: let no one deceive you — the one who practices righteousness is righteous, after the pattern of Christ himself; righteousness is proved by doing, not by mere profession. The verse displays the doer of righteousness, over against the child of the devil (v.8).
8:
"He that committeth sin is of the devil; for the devil sinneth from the beginning. For this purpose the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy the works of the devil." The witnesses note that Christ was manifested to destroy the works of the devil. On "he that committeth sin is of the devil," the grammatical voices note the origin of sin. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the righteous: "he that committeth (practices) sin is of the devil; for the devil sinneth (has sinned) from the beginning" — the one who lives in sin belongs to the devil, who has been a sinner from the start; sin's true parent is the devil, and those who practice it show whose children they are.

On "the devil sinneth from the beginning," the witnesses note Satan's character. Barnes and Calvin note that "the devil sinneth from the beginning" — Satan has been the author and practiser of sin from his fall; he is the source and pattern of all sin; to sin is to imitate and belong to him.

On "the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy the works of the devil," the eastern and grammatical-historical note the purpose of Christ: "for this purpose the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy (loose, undo, dissolve) the works of the devil" — Christ came expressly to undo and destroy all that the devil has done — sin and its effects; the great purpose of his coming was the overthrow of Satan's works. The verse displays that Christ was manifested to destroy the works of the devil.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the origin — "he that committeth (practices) sin is of the devil; for the devil sinneth from the beginning," the one who lives in sin belonging to the devil, sin's true parent (Barnes, Robertson); "the devil sinneth from the beginning," Satan the author and pattern of all sin from his fall (Barnes, Calvin); "the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy the works of the devil," Christ coming to undo all the devil's works, sin and its effects (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the destroyer of the devil's works. The verse marks the sinner: of the devil. The verse displays the devil: sinning from the beginning. The verse displays the purpose: destroy his works. The verse displays the Son: manifested. The teaching displays Christ undoing the devil's works of sin. The verse displays the sin: of the devil. The verse displays the devil: the sinner. The verse displays the works: destroyed. That Christ was manifested to destroy the works of the devil displays the cosmic purpose behind the call to righteousness: the one who practices sin is of the devil, who has sinned from the beginning — but the Son of God appeared expressly to destroy the devil's works; so the children of God break with the sin he came to undo. The verse displays the destroyed works of the devil, replaced by the seed of God (v.9).
9:
"Whosoever is born of God doth not commit sin; for his seed remaineth in him: and he cannot sin, because he is born of God." The witnesses note that the one born of God does not practice sin — the perfection crux. On "whosoever is born of God doth not commit sin... and he cannot sin," interpreters reconcile this with 1:8. The latin-fathers Augustine frames the dilemma: how can John say "whosoever is born of God sinneth not" and also "if we say we have no sin we deceive ourselves"? The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with the Reformed Calvin, resolve it by the sense and tense: "doth not commit sin (hamartian ou poiei — does not practice sin) ... and he cannot sin (ou dynatai hamartanein — cannot go on sinning, the linear present)" means the regenerate does not make a practice of sin, cannot habitually and characteristically sin; not that he never commits a single act (Robertson: "a great deal of false theology has grown out of a misunderstanding of the tense"). Barnes: it means he will not sin so as to lose religion or fall away; the seed makes him not characteristically a sinner. Calvin: the regenerate still have remnants of the old nature and daily sin, but the Spirit so reigns that sin cannot rule.

On "his seed remaineth in him," the witnesses note the abiding principle. Barnes and Calvin note that "his (God's) seed (the germ of divine life, the principle of holiness implanted by the word and Spirit) remaineth (abides) in him" — the incorruptible seed of regeneration abides permanently, restraining sin and securing perseverance; Calvin: though sometimes stifled (as in David), the hidden root remains and springs up again.

On "he cannot sin, because he is born of God," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the victory more fully: while the divine seed of loving faith abides, the believer "cannot sin," being inwardly and universally changed; the new birth gives real mastery over sin, not merely reduced habit; Clarke protests that the "merely habitual" reading sets the grace of Christ too low. (Calvin firmly rejects the antinomian abuse — that the regenerate may sin freely.) The verse displays that the one born of God does not practice sin.

The witnesses agree the substance: the regenerate, having God's abiding seed, does not and cannot live in sin; regeneration breaks the dominion of sin (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley). But the reconciliation with 1:8 divides emphasis: the grammatical/Reformed reading takes "cannot sin" as the habitual present (does not practice sin), the seed making sin unable to reign; the Wesleyan reading takes it as a fuller, real victory over sin while the seed abides. Both reject the antinomian abuse and the sinless-perfection-of-all. The verse displays the perfection crux. The verse marks the birth: of God. The verse displays the seed: remaineth. The verse displays the debate: habitual practice or full victory. The verse displays the reconciliation: with 1:8. The crux displays the regenerate unable to live in sin. The verse displays the born: of God. The verse displays the seed: abiding. The verse displays the sin: not practiced. That the one born of God does not practice sin displays the great perfection crux: the regenerate, in whom God's incorruptible seed abides, does not commit sin and cannot sin — read by the grammatical and Reformed voices as the habitual present (does not practice sin, reconciled with 1:8 by tense; the seed makes sin unable to reign — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin) and by the Wesleyan voices as a real victory over sin while the seed abides (Wesley, Clarke), all rejecting the antinomian abuse. The verse displays the seed of God, marking the children of God from the children of the devil (v.10).
10:
"In this the children of God are manifest, and the children of the devil: whosoever doeth not righteousness is not of God, neither he that loveth not his brother." The witnesses note the twofold test distinguishing God's children from the devil's. On "in this the children of God are manifest, and the children of the devil," the grammatical voices note the dividing line. Barnes and Robertson note the visible distinction: "in this (by this test) the children of God are manifest (made evident), and the children of the devil" — there is a clear mark by which the two families are distinguished; conduct reveals to which family one belongs.

On "whosoever doeth not righteousness is not of God," the witnesses note the first test. Barnes and Calvin note the criterion of righteousness: "whosoever doeth not (practices not) righteousness is not of God" — the absence of righteous living proves one is not God's child; doing righteousness is the mark of the divine birth.

On "neither he that loveth not his brother," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the second test: "neither he that loveth not his brother" — love of the brethren is joined to righteousness as the test; the one who does not love his brother is likewise not of God; the two marks — righteousness and brotherly love — together distinguish the children of God. The verse displays the twofold test distinguishing God's children from the devil's.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dividing line — "in this the children of God are manifest, and the children of the devil," conduct revealing the two families (Barnes, Robertson); "whosoever doeth not righteousness is not of God," the absence of righteous living proving one not God's child (Barnes, Calvin); "neither he that loveth not his brother," brotherly love joined to righteousness as the test (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the twofold test. The verse marks the manifestation: the two families. The verse displays the first test: righteousness. The verse displays the second test: brotherly love. The verse displays the division: of God or the devil. The test displays righteousness and love marking God's children. The verse displays the children: manifest. The verse displays the righteousness: required. The verse displays the love: required. The twofold test distinguishing God's children from the devil's displays the dividing line of the chapter: by this the two families are made manifest — whoever does not practice righteousness, nor loves his brother, is not of God; righteousness and brotherly love together mark the children of God. The verse displays the test of love, the message from the beginning (v.11).
11:
"For this is the message that ye heard from the beginning, that we should love one another." The brief verse notes the message from the beginning: to love one another. The grammatical voices note the ancient command. Barnes and Robertson note the foundational charge: "this is the message (angelia — the announcement, the gospel command) that ye heard from the beginning (from the first preaching of the gospel), that we should love one another" — the command to mutual love is no new thing but the message heard from the start of their Christian instruction.

On "the message that ye heard from the beginning," the witnesses note its antiquity. Barnes and Calvin note that this love was commanded "from the beginning" — it is fundamental to the gospel, taught from the first; not a novelty but the heart of Christ's teaching.

On "that we should love one another," the puritan Henry notes the substance: the message is "that we should love one another" — mutual love among believers is the great command, the sum of the second table, the badge of Christ's disciples (John 13:34-35); the chapter's theme of brotherly love is grounded in this foundational message. The verse displays the message from the beginning: to love one another.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ancient command — "this is the message that ye heard from the beginning, that we should love one another," the command to mutual love heard from the first of their instruction (Barnes, Robertson); commanded "from the beginning," fundamental to the gospel, not a novelty (Barnes, Calvin); "that we should love one another," the great command, the badge of Christ's disciples (John 13:34-35, Henry). The verse displays the message of love. The verse marks the message: from the beginning. The verse displays the command: love one another. The verse displays the antiquity: from the first. The verse displays the substance: mutual love. The message displays brotherly love as the foundational charge. The verse displays the message: heard. The verse displays the love: commanded. The verse displays the beginning: from the first. The message from the beginning — to love one another — displays the ground of the chapter's theme: the command to mutual love is the message heard from the first, fundamental to the gospel and the badge of Christ's disciples. The verse displays the command of love, against the example of Cain (v.12).
12:
"Not as Cain, who was of that wicked one, and slew his brother. And wherefore slew he him? Because his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous." The witnesses note Cain the murderer as the warning against brother-hatred. On "Not as Cain, who was of that wicked one, and slew his brother," the grammatical voices note the negative example. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast to love: "not as Cain, who was of that wicked one (the devil), and slew (murdered) his brother" — Cain is the opposite of brotherly love; belonging to the devil, he murdered Abel; the type of hatred carried to its end.

On "of that wicked one," the witnesses note Cain's parentage. Barnes and Calvin note that Cain "was of that wicked one (the devil)" — he belonged to and was governed by the devil; his murder showed whose child he was; hatred has its father in Satan.

On "his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous," the puritan Henry notes the cause of the hatred: Cain slew Abel "because his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous" — the deepest cause of his hatred was the contrast between his evil works and Abel's righteousness; the wicked hate the righteous because their goodness rebukes the evil; envy of righteousness drove the murder. The verse displays Cain the murderer as the warning against brother-hatred.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the negative example — "not as Cain, who was of that wicked one (the devil), and slew his brother," Cain the opposite of love, murdering Abel as the devil's child (Barnes, Robertson); "of that wicked one," Cain belonging to and governed by the devil, hatred fathered by Satan (Barnes, Calvin); "his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous," the wicked hating the righteous whose goodness rebukes them, envy driving the murder (Henry). The verse displays the warning of Cain. The verse marks the example: not as Cain. The verse displays the parentage: of the wicked one. The verse displays the deed: slew his brother. The verse displays the cause: envy of righteousness. The example displays hatred carried to murder by the devil's child. The verse displays the Cain: murderer. The verse displays the works: evil. The verse displays the brother: righteous. Cain the murderer as the warning against brother-hatred displays the dark contrast to brotherly love: not like Cain, who, being of the devil, murdered his brother because his own works were evil and his brother's righteous — hatred fathered by Satan and driven by envy of the good. The verse displays the warning of Cain, against the world's hatred of believers (v.13).
13:
"Marvel not, my brethren, if the world hate you." The brief verse notes that believers should not marvel at the world's hatred. The grammatical voices note the expected hostility. Barnes and Robertson note the counsel against surprise: "marvel not (do not be astonished), my brethren, if the world hate you" — the believers are not to be surprised when the world hates them; the world's hatred of the godly is to be expected, as Cain's hatred of Abel (v.12) shows.

On "if the world hate you," the witnesses note the cause. Barnes and Calvin note that the world hates believers for the same reason Cain hated Abel — the contrast between the world's evil and the believers' righteousness; the godly life rebukes the world, which responds with hatred.

On "marvel not," the puritan Henry notes the comfort: the command "marvel not" steadies the believer — knowing that the world's hatred is the normal lot of the righteous, he is neither surprised nor discouraged by it; it is a mark of belonging to Christ, not a sign of God's disfavour. The verse displays that believers should not marvel at the world's hatred.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the counsel — "marvel not (be not astonished), my brethren, if the world hate you," not to be surprised at the world's hatred, expected as Cain's of Abel (Barnes, Robertson); the world hating believers for their righteousness, which rebukes its evil (Barnes, Calvin); "marvel not," steadying the believer, hatred the normal lot of the righteous, not God's disfavour (Henry). The verse displays the expected hatred. The verse marks the counsel: marvel not. The verse displays the source: the world. The verse displays the cause: righteousness rebukes evil. The verse displays the comfort: the normal lot. The counsel displays the believer unsurprised by the world's hatred. The verse displays the marveling: forbidden. The verse displays the world: hating. The verse displays the brethren: hated. That believers should not marvel at the world's hatred displays the expected cost of righteousness: do not be astonished, brethren, if the world hates you — for the world's hatred of the godly, like Cain's of Abel, is the normal lot of those whose righteousness rebukes its evil. The verse displays the world's hatred, contrasted with the believers' passage to life (v.14).
14:
"We know that we have passed from death unto life, because we love the brethren. He that loveth not his brother abideth in death." The witnesses note that love of the brethren is the evidence of having passed from death to life. On "we know that we have passed from death unto life, because we love the brethren," the grammatical voices note the test of assurance. Barnes and Robertson note the mark of the new life: "we know (have assurance) that we have passed (been transferred) from death unto life, because we love the brethren" — love of fellow-believers is the evidence by which we know we have passed from spiritual death to spiritual life; brotherly love is the proof and fruit of regeneration.

On "because we love the brethren," the witnesses note the evidence. Barnes and Calvin note that love of the brethren is not the cause but the evidence of the new life; where genuine love for God's people exists, there is proof of having passed into life; love is the surest sign of regeneration.

On "he that loveth not his brother abideth in death," the eastern Cyril notes the converse: "he that loveth not his brother abideth (remains) in death" — the one without brotherly love remains in spiritual death; the absence of love proves the absence of life; loveless religion is dead. The verse displays that love of the brethren is the evidence of having passed from death to life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the test — "we know that we have passed from death unto life, because we love the brethren," brotherly love the evidence of having passed from death to life (Barnes, Robertson); love the evidence not the cause of the new life, the surest sign of regeneration (Barnes, Calvin); "he that loveth not his brother abideth in death," the loveless remaining in spiritual death (Cyril). The verse displays the evidence of life. The verse marks the assurance: we know. The verse displays the passage: death to life. The verse displays the evidence: love of the brethren. The verse displays the converse: lovelessness is death. The test displays brotherly love proving the new life. The verse displays the life: attained. The verse displays the love: the proof. The verse displays the loveless: in death. That love of the brethren is the evidence of having passed from death to life displays the great test of assurance: we know we have passed from death to life because we love the brethren — while the one who does not love his brother remains in death. The verse displays the evidence of love, intensified by the gravity of hatred (v.15).
15:
"Whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer: and ye know that no murderer hath eternal life abiding in him." The witnesses note that hatred is murder, and no murderer has eternal life. On "whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer," the grammatical voices note the equation. Barnes and Robertson note the searching judgment: "whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer (anthropoktonos — a manslayer)" — hatred is, in God's sight, equivalent to murder; the one who hates his brother is a murderer at heart, for hatred is the root of which murder is the fruit (as Cain showed); God judges the inward disposition.

On "hateth his brother is a murderer," the witnesses note the inward sin. Barnes and Calvin note that hatred, though it never proceeds to the act of killing, is murder in the heart; God looks at the disposition, not merely the deed; the hater bears the guilt of the murderer in principle (cf. Matthew 5:21-22).

On "no murderer hath eternal life abiding in him," the puritan Henry notes the consequence: "ye know that no murderer hath eternal life abiding in him" — the murderer (and so the hater) does not have eternal life dwelling in him; hatred and eternal life are incompatible; the presence of hatred proves the absence of the new life. The verse displays that hatred is murder, and no murderer has eternal life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the equation — "whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer (manslayer)," hatred equivalent to murder in God's sight, the root of which murder is the fruit (Barnes, Robertson); hatred murder in the heart, God judging the disposition not merely the deed (Matthew 5:21-22, Barnes, Calvin); "no murderer hath eternal life abiding in him," hatred and eternal life incompatible, hatred proving the absence of life (Henry). The verse displays the gravity of hatred. The verse marks the hatred: is murder. The verse displays the judgment: of the heart. The verse displays the consequence: no eternal life. The verse displays the incompatibility: hatred and life. The teaching displays hatred as murder excluding eternal life. The verse displays the hater: a murderer. The verse displays the murder: in the heart. The verse displays the life: absent. That hatred is murder, and no murderer has eternal life, displays the searching gravity of brother-hatred: whoever hates his brother is a murderer at heart, and no murderer has eternal life abiding in him — hatred and the new life cannot coexist. The verse displays the murderous hatred, set against the self-giving love of Christ (v.16).
16:
"Hereby perceive we the love of God, because he laid down his life for us: and we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren." The witnesses note the love of Christ in laying down his life, the pattern for ours. On "hereby perceive we the love of God, because he laid down his life for us," the grammatical voices note the supreme demonstration. Barnes and Robertson note the measure of love: "hereby (by this) perceive we the love (of God, or of Christ), because he laid down his life for us" — the love of God (in Christ) is known supremely in this, that Christ laid down his life for us; the cross is the definition and proof of love.

On "he laid down his life for us," the witnesses note the self-sacrifice. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ's "laying down his life for us" is the highest possible act of love — the voluntary giving of his life for sinners; love has no greater expression (John 15:13).

On "we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren," the puritan Henry notes the obligation: "we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren" — Christ's self-giving love lays an obligation on believers to love sacrificially in return, even to the giving of their lives for fellow-believers; the cross is not only the proof but the pattern of love. The verse displays the love of Christ in laying down his life, the pattern for ours.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the demonstration — "hereby perceive we the love... because he laid down his life for us," the love of God known supremely in Christ's laying down his life, the cross the definition of love (Barnes, Robertson); Christ's self-sacrifice the highest act of love, for sinners (John 15:13, Barnes, Calvin); "we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren," the cross laying an obligation of sacrificial love, the pattern for ours (Henry). The verse displays the self-giving love. The verse marks the love: perceived. The verse displays the proof: he laid down his life. The verse displays the obligation: lay down ours. The verse displays the pattern: Christ's sacrifice. The love displays Christ's self-giving as the model for ours. The verse displays the love: of Christ. The verse displays the life: laid down. The verse displays the brethren: loved. The love of Christ in laying down his life, the pattern for ours, displays the supreme measure of love: we know love by this, that Christ laid down his life for us — and we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren, the cross both the proof and the pattern of sacrificial love. The verse displays the self-giving love, tested by deeds toward the needy (v.17).
17:
"But whoso hath this world's good, and seeth his brother have need, and shutteth up his bowels of compassion from him, how dwelleth the love of God in him?" The witnesses note that withholding help from the needy brother proves the absence of God's love. On "whoso hath this world's good, and seeth his brother have need," the grammatical voices note the test of practical love. Barnes and Robertson note the searching case: "whoso hath this world's good (the means of this life, material possessions), and seeth his brother have need (in want)" — the one who possesses the means to help and sees a fellow-believer in genuine need is put to the test of love.

On "shutteth up his bowels of compassion from him," the witnesses note the closed heart. Barnes and Calvin note the failure: "shutteth up his bowels of compassion (his heart, the seat of tender affection) from him" — he closes his heart against the needy brother, withholding the compassion and help he could give; the deliberate refusal of mercy.

On "how dwelleth the love of God in him?" the puritan Henry notes the verdict: "how dwelleth the love of God in him?" — the question expects the answer "it does not"; the one who withholds help from the needy brother gives proof that the love of God does not dwell in him; loveless to the brother, he is loveless toward God; deeds, not words, prove love. The verse displays that withholding help from the needy brother proves the absence of God's love.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the test — "whoso hath this world's good, and seeth his brother have need," the one with means seeing a brother in want put to the test (Barnes, Robertson); "shutteth up his bowels of compassion from him," closing his heart against the needy, refusing mercy (Barnes, Calvin); "how dwelleth the love of God in him?" the withholder proving God's love does not dwell in him, deeds proving love (Henry). The verse displays the test of deeds. The verse marks the means: this world's good. The verse displays the need: the brother's. The verse displays the failure: shut-up compassion. The verse displays the verdict: no love of God. The test displays love proved or disproved by helping the needy. The verse displays the good: possessed. The verse displays the compassion: withheld. The verse displays the love: absent. That withholding help from the needy brother proves the absence of God's love displays the practical test of love: the one who has the means, sees his brother in need, and closes his heart against him — how does the love of God dwell in him? It does not; love is proved by deeds of mercy, not by profession. The verse displays the test of deeds, demanding love in deed and truth (v.18).
18:
"My little children, let us not love in word, neither in tongue; but in deed and in truth." The brief verse notes the call to love in deed and truth, not merely in word. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation flowing from v.17: "let us not love in word (mere speech), neither in tongue (mere talk); but in deed (action) and in truth (genuine reality)" — love is not to be confined to words but expressed in real action and sincere reality; the contrast between professed and practiced love.

On "in word, neither in tongue," the witnesses note the empty profession. Barnes and Calvin note that "love in word and tongue" is mere verbal profession — fine words of love without corresponding deeds; such love is empty and false; many who say much love little.

On "in deed and in truth," the puritan Henry notes the genuine love: real love is "in deed (in action, by helping) and in truth (sincerely, genuinely)" — love proved by deeds and springing from a true heart; the only love that is real is the love that acts; deeds and sincerity are the marks of true love. The verse displays the call to love in deed and truth, not merely in word.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "let us not love in word, neither in tongue; but in deed and in truth," love not confined to words but expressed in real action and sincerity (Barnes, Robertson); "love in word and tongue" mere verbal profession, empty without deeds (Barnes, Calvin); "in deed and in truth," love proved by action and springing from a true heart (Henry). The verse displays the genuine love. The verse marks the negative: not in word. The verse displays the positive: in deed and truth. The verse displays the empty: mere talk. The verse displays the real: action and sincerity. The call displays love that acts, not merely speaks. The verse displays the word: insufficient. The verse displays the deed: required. The verse displays the truth: sincere. The call to love in deed and truth, not merely in word, displays the practical conclusion of the chapter's teaching on love: let us not love with mere words and talk, but with real action and genuine sincerity — for only the love that acts is true love. The verse displays the love in deed, the ground of an assured heart (vv.19-20).
19:
"And hereby we know that we are of the truth, and shall assure our hearts before him." The brief verse notes that love in deed assures our hearts that we are of the truth. The grammatical voices note the ground of assurance. Barnes and Robertson note the fruit of genuine love: "hereby (by this, by loving in deed and truth, v.18) we know that we are of the truth (belong to the truth, are genuine Christians), and shall assure (persuade, set at rest) our hearts before him" — practical love is the evidence by which we know we belong to the truth, and it sets our hearts at rest before God.

On "we are of the truth," the witnesses note the genuine standing. Barnes and Calvin note that to be "of the truth" is to be a true Christian, born of and belonging to the truth; love in deed is the proof of this genuine standing; the test of v.18 yields assurance.

On "shall assure our hearts before him," the latin-fathers Augustine notes the quieting of the heart: by genuine love we "assure (persuade, quiet) our hearts before him (in God's presence)" — the practice of real love calms and reassures the conscience before God; where love is genuine, the heart finds rest and confidence in God's sight. The verse displays that love in deed assures our hearts that we are of the truth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the assurance — "hereby we know that we are of the truth, and shall assure our hearts before him," practical love the evidence of belonging to the truth, setting the heart at rest (Barnes, Robertson); to be "of the truth" the genuine Christian standing, proved by love in deed (Barnes, Calvin); "assure our hearts before him," real love quieting the conscience before God (Augustine). The verse displays the assured heart. The verse marks the knowledge: of the truth. The verse displays the evidence: love in deed. The verse displays the effect: assure our hearts. The verse displays the place: before him. The assurance displays genuine love quieting the heart before God. The verse displays the truth: belonged to. The verse displays the heart: assured. The verse displays the God: before whom. That love in deed assures our hearts that we are of the truth displays the comfort flowing from genuine love: by loving in deed and truth we know we belong to the truth, and our hearts are set at rest before God. The verse displays the assured heart, even when conscience accuses (v.20).
20:
"For if our heart condemn us, God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things." The witnesses note God greater than our condemning heart, knowing all things. On "if our heart condemn us," the grammatical voices note the accusing conscience. Barnes and Robertson note the case of self-condemnation: "if our heart condemn us (our conscience accuses, reproaches us)" — the believer's own heart may at times accuse and condemn him; the troubled conscience that doubts its sincerity.

On "God is greater than our heart," interpreters note the sense. Barnes and Calvin note two readings of "God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things." On one reading (comforting), God, knowing all things, knows the genuine love and sincerity the troubled heart may overlook; he is a more competent and merciful judge than our own anxious conscience, and where there is real love (v.19), his fuller knowledge reassures. On another reading (solemn), if our heart condemns us, much more does God, who is greater and knows all, condemn — so a self-condemning heart is a fearful thing before the all-knowing God.

On "knoweth all things," the puritan Henry notes the omniscience: God "knoweth all things" — his knowledge is complete, searching the heart fully; whether for comfort (he sees the sincerity we doubt) or for solemnity (he sees the sin we hide), his omniscience is the ground; the context of assurance (vv.19, 21) favours the comforting sense. The verse displays God greater than our condemning heart, knowing all things.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree the substance: God, greater than the heart and knowing all things, is the final judge of our standing; his omniscience bears on the accusing conscience (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry). Interpreters note the two readings: the comforting (God's fuller knowledge reassures where there is real love) and the solemn (God condemns more than the heart); the context of assurance favours the comforting sense, while the solemn sense is acknowledged. The verse displays the all-knowing God. The verse marks the heart: condemning. The verse displays the God: greater. The verse displays the omniscience: knows all. The verse displays the reading: comfort or solemnity. The teaching displays God's full knowledge bearing on the troubled heart. The verse displays the heart: accusing. The verse displays the God: greater. The verse displays the knowledge: complete. God greater than our condemning heart, knowing all things, displays the bearing of God's omniscience on the accusing conscience: if our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart and knows all — read for comfort (he sees the genuine love our anxious heart overlooks) or for solemnity (he, knowing all, judges more than we do), the context of assurance favouring the comforting sense. The verse displays the all-knowing God, before whom the unaccused heart has confidence (v.21).
21:
"Beloved, if our heart condemn us not, then have we confidence toward God." The brief verse notes the confidence toward God of the heart that does not condemn. The grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note the converse of v.20: "if our heart condemn us not (does not accuse us, when conscience is clear), then have we confidence (parresia — boldness, freedom of access) toward God" — when the conscience, made clear by genuine love and obedience, does not condemn, the believer has boldness and confidence before God.

On "if our heart condemn us not," the witnesses note the clear conscience. Barnes and Calvin note that a heart that does not condemn is one cleansed and reassured by sincere love and faith; not a heart that has no sin, but one resting in genuine grace, not reproaching itself with hypocrisy or unlove.

On "confidence toward God," the puritan Henry notes the boldness: such a heart has "confidence (boldness) toward God" — freedom and assurance in approaching God, especially in prayer; the clear conscience emboldens the believer to draw near and to ask; assurance issues in confident access. The verse displays the confidence toward God of the heart that does not condemn.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the assurance — "if our heart condemn us not, then have we confidence (boldness) toward God," the clear conscience giving boldness and access before God (Barnes, Robertson); a heart that does not condemn one cleansed by sincere love and faith, resting in grace (Barnes, Calvin); "confidence toward God," boldness in approaching God, especially in prayer (Henry). The verse displays the confident heart. The verse marks the heart: not condemning. The verse displays the result: confidence. The verse displays the access: toward God. The verse displays the ground: clear conscience. The assurance displays the clear conscience emboldening approach to God. The verse displays the heart: clear. The verse displays the confidence: held. The verse displays the God: approached. The confidence toward God of the heart that does not condemn displays the fruit of an assured conscience: when our heart, cleansed by genuine love and obedience, does not condemn us, we have boldness and confidence toward God. The verse displays the confident heart, whose prayers are answered (v.22).
22:
"And whatsoever we ask, we receive of him, because we keep his commandments, and do those things that are pleasing in his sight." The witnesses note answered prayer joined to obedience and pleasing God. On "whatsoever we ask, we receive of him," the grammatical voices note the promise. Barnes and Robertson note the assurance of answered prayer: "whatsoever we ask, we receive of him" — the believer with a clear conscience and confidence (v.21) receives what he asks of God; the promise of answered prayer (to be understood, as elsewhere, of asking according to God's will).

On "because we keep his commandments," the witnesses note the condition. Barnes and Calvin note the ground: "because we keep (observe) his commandments" — answered prayer is joined to obedience; the obedient believer, walking in God's commands, is the one whose prayers are heard; not that obedience earns the answer, but that it is the mark of the one in fellowship with God whose asking accords with his will.

On "do those things that are pleasing in his sight," the puritan Henry notes the pleasing life: "do those things that are pleasing in his sight" — beyond bare obedience, the believer seeks to please God in all things; the life that aims at God's pleasure is the life whose prayers prevail; a heart set on pleasing God asks in harmony with God's will and receives. The verse displays answered prayer joined to obedience and pleasing God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "whatsoever we ask, we receive of him," the believer with a clear conscience receiving what he asks (according to God's will, Barnes, Robertson); "because we keep his commandments," answered prayer joined to obedience, the obedient heard, obedience not earning but marking fellowship (Barnes, Calvin); "do those things that are pleasing in his sight," the life aimed at pleasing God whose prayers prevail (Henry). The verse displays answered prayer. The verse marks the asking: whatsoever. The verse displays the receiving: of him. The verse displays the condition: keep his commandments. The verse displays the life: pleasing to God. The promise displays the obedient, pleasing believer's prayers answered. The verse displays the prayer: heard. The verse displays the obedience: kept. The verse displays the pleasing: sought. Answered prayer joined to obedience and pleasing God displays the privilege of the assured heart: whatever we ask we receive of him, because we keep his commandments and do what pleases him — the obedient life, aimed at God's pleasure, asking in harmony with his will and receiving. The verse displays the answered prayer, grounded in faith and love (v.23).
23:
"And this is his commandment, That we should believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ, and love one another, as he gave us commandment." The witnesses note the twofold commandment: to believe on Christ and to love one another. On "this is his commandment, That we should believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the command to faith. Barnes and Robertson note the first part: "this is his commandment, That we should believe on (put faith in) the name of his Son Jesus Christ" — God commands faith in his Son; believing on Christ's name (his person and work) is itself a commandment, the first and fundamental duty.

On "and love one another," the witnesses note the command to love. Barnes and Calvin note the second part: "and love one another" — joined to faith is the command to mutual love; faith and love together sum up the commandment; the two are inseparable, faith toward Christ and love toward the brethren.

On "as he gave us commandment," the eastern Cyril notes the divine authority: this love is "as he gave us commandment" — Christ himself commanded it (John 13:34); the love is not optional but commanded, on the authority of Christ; faith and love together are the substance of God's commandment. The verse displays the twofold commandment: to believe on Christ and to love one another.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command to faith — "this is his commandment, That we should believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ," God commanding faith in his Son, the fundamental duty (Barnes, Robertson); "and love one another," mutual love joined to faith, the two inseparable (Barnes, Calvin); "as he gave us commandment," the love commanded by Christ himself (John 13:34, Cyril). The verse displays the twofold command. The verse marks the faith: believe on Christ. The verse displays the love: one another. The verse displays the union: faith and love. The verse displays the authority: Christ's command. The commandment displays faith and love as the sum of God's command. The verse displays the believing: commanded. The verse displays the loving: commanded. The verse displays the Son: believed on. The twofold commandment — to believe on Christ and to love one another — displays the sum of God's command: that we believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ and love one another, as Christ commanded; faith toward the Son and love toward the brethren, inseparably joined, are the substance of the commandment. The verse displays the command of faith and love, kept by those who abide in God (v.24).
24:
"And he that keepeth his commandments dwelleth in him, and he in him. And hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us." The witnesses note the mutual abiding of God and the believer, known by the Spirit. On "he that keepeth his commandments dwelleth in him, and he in him," the grammatical voices note the mutual indwelling. Barnes and Robertson note the reciprocal abiding: "he that keepeth (observes) his commandments dwelleth (abides) in him, and he (God) in him" — the obedient believer abides in God, and God abides in him; the keeping of God's commandments (faith and love, v.23) is the mark and means of this mutual indwelling.

On "dwelleth in him, and he in him," the witnesses note the union. Barnes and Calvin note the intimate fellowship: the believer and God dwell in each other; a real, reciprocal communion, the believer in God and God in the believer; obedience is the evidence of this union.

On "hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us," the eastern and grammatical-historical note the witness of the Spirit: "hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us" — the proof of God's abiding presence is the Holy Spirit he has given; the indwelling Spirit is the witness and seal of the union, the inward evidence that God dwells in us. The verse displays the mutual abiding of God and the believer, known by the Spirit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mutual indwelling — "he that keepeth his commandments dwelleth in him, and he in him," the obedient believer abiding in God and God in him, obedience the mark (Barnes, Robertson); the intimate reciprocal communion, obedience the evidence (Barnes, Calvin); "hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us," the indwelling Spirit the witness and seal of the union (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mutual abiding. The verse marks the keeping: his commandments. The verse displays the indwelling: he in him, he in him. The verse displays the witness: the Spirit. The verse displays the evidence: the gift of the Spirit. The abiding displays God and the believer dwelling in each other by the Spirit. The verse displays the abiding: mutual. The verse displays the Spirit: given. The verse displays the union: known. The mutual abiding of God and the believer, known by the Spirit, displays the crown of the chapter: the one who keeps God's commandments dwells in him and he in him — and we know that God abides in us by the Spirit he has given us, the indwelling Spirit the witness of the union. The verse displays the abiding Spirit, by whom the spirits are to be tested (ch.4).
